question: Would Jackie Chan have trouble communicating with a deaf person?

debater_a.brief_reasoning: Jackie Chan is known to speak multiple languages and has extensive experience communicating across language barriers in international film work. Communicating with a deaf person can be done effectively through writing, gestures, and basic sign or visual cues, so it’s not inherently something he would have trouble with. While he may not know sign language, that doesn’t imply he would struggle more than an average person.

debater_b.brief_reasoning: Jackie Chan primarily communicates through spoken languages like Cantonese, Mandarin, and English, which a deaf person may not be able to hear. Unless he and the deaf person share a common sign language or use writing/gestures, communication would likely be difficult. Deaf people can lip-read or use interpreters, but that still often makes communication harder than with a hearing person.

debater_a.argument: A deaf person cannot hear spoken language, but communication can still happen through visual means like gestures, writing, or sign language. Jackie Chan is widely known for expressive physical acting and pantomime in his films, which are effective tools for nonverbal communication. Therefore, he would not necessarily have trouble communicating with a deaf person, since he can rely on clear gestures and written communication.
debater_a.rebuttal: There is no opponent point yet to rebut; if the opponent argues he doesn’t know sign language, that doesn’t imply he’d have trouble overall because writing and gestures can still work.

debater_b.argument: A deaf person cannot access spoken language, and most hearing people—including Jackie Chan—are not known to be fluent in sign language. While gestures and writing can help, they are often insufficient for nuanced, rapid, or complex conversation, especially with someone who primarily uses a signed language. Jackie Chan’s expressive acting is performance-oriented and doesn’t imply competence in a deaf person’s preferred communication mode. So he would likely have some trouble communicating compared to speaking with a hearing person.
debater_b.rebuttal: Debater A conflates being good at pantomime with being able to communicate effectively in real-life conversation. Gestures and writing are workarounds, but they typically make communication slower and less precise, which still counts as “having trouble.”

debater_a.argument: Communicating with a deaf person does not inherently require spoken language; many interactions can be handled effectively through writing, texting, lip-reading support, and clear gestures. Jackie Chan in particular is known for highly expressive, visually clear communication through body language, which is directly useful in face-to-face nonverbal exchange. The question asks whether he would have trouble, and it’s plausible he could communicate adequately using these common alternatives without major difficulty. Therefore, it’s reasonable to answer that he would not necessarily have trouble communicating with a deaf person.
debater_a.rebuttal: Lack of sign-language fluency doesn’t imply he’d have trouble in general, because many deaf people communicate via writing/texting and contextual gestures with hearing people routinely. “More effort than speaking” is not the same as “having trouble” in the sense of being unable to communicate; basic effective communication can still proceed smoothly via visual channels.

debater_b.argument: Most deaf people cannot access spoken Mandarin/Cantonese/English directly, and Jackie Chan is not publicly known to be fluent in any signed language. While writing, texting, and ad‑hoc gestures can work for simple exchanges, they typically slow interaction and reduce precision for anything nuanced or fast-paced. Since the question asks whether he would have trouble (not whether communication is impossible), the likely need to rely on imperfect workarounds implies some trouble compared to communicating with a hearing person.
debater_b.rebuttal: Debater A sets the bar at “can communicate at all,” but “would have trouble” includes added friction, misunderstandings, and slower back-and-forth. Expressive acting and generic gestures don’t substitute for shared language (e.g., sign) and don’t guarantee smooth real-world conversation with a deaf person.

debater_a.argument: A deaf person’s inability to hear speech doesn’t prevent communication; it shifts it to visual channels like writing/texting, pointing, and conventional gestures. In everyday contexts, many deaf–hearing interactions proceed effectively without shared sign-language fluency, especially for practical topics. Jackie Chan is exceptionally adept at clear, intentional nonverbal expression, which would likely make basic face-to-face communication easier rather than harder. So it’s reasonable to say he would not have trouble communicating in the general sense of being able to convey and understand messages.
debater_a.rebuttal: The opponent’s standard treats any added friction versus hearing-to-hearing speech as “trouble,” but the question is not explicitly comparative; it asks whether he would have trouble at all. Since common tools like writing/texting can make communication straightforward, lack of sign fluency doesn’t imply he’d struggle to communicate effectively.

debater_b.argument: Yes. Most deaf people primarily use a signed language or a mix of signing, lip-reading, and writing, and Jackie Chan is not generally known to be fluent in any sign language. Without a shared language, communication often becomes slower and more error-prone, relying on ad-hoc gestures and writing that work best for simple, concrete topics. Since the question asks whether he would have trouble (not whether it’s impossible), the likely need for these workarounds implies he would have some trouble communicating.
debater_b.rebuttal: Debater A equates “can communicate somehow” with “no trouble,” but needing to switch to writing/gestures and losing speed/nuance is exactly what “having trouble communicating” commonly means. Being expressive on screen doesn’t ensure mutual understanding in real conversations without shared sign language or established conventions.

debater_a.argument: No, because communicating with a deaf person is often straightforward via visual channels like writing/texting, pointing, and conventional gestures, even when neither party knows sign language. The question is about whether he would have trouble communicating at all, not whether it would be identical in speed to spoken conversation. Jackie Chan’s well-known ability to convey meaning through clear physical expression would likely make basic in-person communication easier rather than harder. For many everyday interactions, these methods are sufficient to communicate effectively without major difficulty.
debater_a.rebuttal: The opponent treats any slowdown versus speech as “trouble,” but that’s a comparative standard the question doesn’t require. Needing to use writing or texting is a common, reliable alternative that often works smoothly, so it doesn’t imply he would have notable difficulty communicating.

debater_b.argument: Yes. Absent shared sign-language fluency, a hearing person will usually face barriers with a deaf person, especially for anything beyond simple, concrete exchanges. Jackie Chan’s on-screen physical expressiveness doesn’t establish that he can understand a deaf person’s responses (signing, limited lip-reading, or written shorthand), which is essential for two-way communication. Relying on writing/texting and improvised gestures typically makes interaction slower and more error-prone, so it’s reasonable to say he would have trouble communicating.
debater_b.rebuttal: The question is about “trouble communicating,” not “total inability,” and needing to switch to workarounds like writing/gestures is itself a common form of trouble (loss of speed, nuance, and mutual comprehension). Also, Jackie Chan’s ability to convey meaning doesn’t solve the harder half of the problem: accurately receiving and interpreting what the deaf person communicates back.

judge.analysis: Debater B was more persuasive because they focused on the ordinary meaning of “have trouble communicating” as including added friction, slower pace, and loss of nuance, not just total inability to exchange any information. B also highlighted the two-way nature of communication: Jackie Chan’s expressiveness might help him convey messages, but it doesn’t ensure he can accurately understand a deaf person’s preferred output (signing, limited lip-reading, etc.) without shared sign language or an interpreter. Debater A’s case leaned heavily on the availability of workarounds (writing/gestures) and on Jackie Chan’s physical acting, but it didn’t convincingly show that these would eliminate “trouble” rather than merely mitigate it. A also relied on a narrower interpretation of “trouble” (nearly impossible vs. merely harder), which is less consistent with common usage in StrategyQA-style questions. Overall, B’s interpretation and reasoning better matched typical expectations about hearing–deaf communication barriers absent sign-language fluency.
judge.strongest_a: Communication can still occur via writing/texting and gestures, so it’s not inherently impossible.
judge.weakest_a: Equates ‘can communicate somehow’ with ‘no trouble,’ and overstates relevance of on-screen pantomime to real two-way conversation.
judge.strongest_b: Even with workarounds, lack of shared sign language usually makes communication slower and more error-prone, which counts as ‘trouble.’
judge.weakest_b: Assumes typical deaf communication preferences without specific evidence about the particular deaf person or Jackie Chan’s actual sign ability.
